entry.title: How to Use Omniauth + Devise on Rails5 From Scratch
entry.content:
This post is no-brainer step by step memo to use omniauth and devise on rails5 for setting up oauth webapp with facebook.

0. env & requiement

Obtain facebook AppID and AppSecret from https://developers.facebook.com/

1. initial steps

create rails app

modify Gemfile and add devise and omniauth

If thor-0.19.2 causes error here, just uninstall thor-0.19.2 and use thor-0.19.1

2. create User model & set facebook AppID and AppSecret

Create User model and add omniauth

set facebook AppID and AppSecret

modify app/models/user.rb and set omniauth module

set path config/routes.rb and confirm

3. create Callback

4. add account setting methods to User model

5. make view

welcome/home is signin portal. home/index tells you if you are signed in or not.

modify layout

6. set root view

add root path and user_root

7. insert authenticate_user! method to welcome/home

“before_action :authenticate_user!” will make the login view automatically.

8. boot and check if webapp works

and go check http://localhost:3000/welcome/home . Click “Sign in with Facebook” and check if it works.

entry.title: Behavior of Hbase Read/Write Requests Counter
entry.content:
HBase has Read/Write Requests System Counter feature and we can monitor it from HBase Web UI or API. Against my instincts, the numbers of those counters could DECREASE while running. This article covers how we could see the decrease of those counters.

We can see read/write request counters of HBase from
http://master-server-node:60010/master-status#requestStats
or
http://region-server-nodes:60010/jmx

These numbers are calculated by summing each region’s counts. Check the iteration part of the code. https://github.com/apache/hbase/blob/947ea85b0c086a022d7b50868b8499a0560331ad/hbase-server/src/main/java/org/apache/hadoop/hbase/regionserver/MetricsRegionServerWrapperImpl.java#L495

So, every time regions get moved or deleted, the number of counters decreases.

Here is a sample check the behaviour.

  1. check http://master-server-node:60010/master-status#requestStats
  2. create table and put some record 
    • $ hbase shell   
hbase> create 'weblog2', 'stats'
hbase> put 'weblog2', 'row1', 'stats:weekly', 'test-weekly-value'
hbase> put 'weblog2', 'row1', 'stats:weekly', 'test-weekly-value'
hbase> put 'weblog2', 'row2', 'stats:daily', 'test-weekly-value'
hbase> put 'weblog2', 'row3', 'stats:monthly', 'test-weekly-value'
  3. reload the above web page and identify which node increases the write counter. That node has the region.
  4. now, delete the table and compare how the value changed
    • check the counter
  • disable and drop the table
  • check the counter again

This happens when you move the Region, too.

The counter values are not migrated when region moved. All counters for the region reset to 0.

Notice in the example above, “totalRequestCount” is not reset to 0. “totalRequestCount” is maintained by an instance variable managed by RegionServer, not Region. Every time RegionServer accepts RPC call from clients, RegionServer increments the count. Here’s one example from the code. Notice requestCount.increment() is called every time RPC call method is called.

https://github.com/apache/hbase/blob/dfb2a800c43b30c326dbdcec673387f7033f2092/hbase-server/src/main/java/org/apache/hadoop/hbase/regionserver/RSRpcServices.java#L1377

When you restart the region server, not only read/write counters but also totalRequestCount are also reset to 0.

entry.title: Install Npm Package From Npm Pack-ed File Using Ansible
entry.content:
In case you want to install npm package file, created by “npm pack” command, just specify the path to the file to “name”.

That’s it.

entry.title: Use Extra-vars of Ansible From Packer
entry.content:
ansible’s “–extra-vars” is the option to pass variables from command line. In using packer with ansible, you can still specify “–extra-vars” through packer’s “extra_arguments”. Here’some example.

  • ansible

We consider the case that we specify parameter “base_ami”, “parameter_1”, and “parameter_2” for ansible.

  • packer

Then, we use those parameters above in packer’s json.

Now you can pass your ansible parameter from packer like this

entry.title: Deploy Rails Websocket App Into Heroku
entry.content:
This post is to explain the configuration example to use heroku to deploy app with websocket-rails. The app is implemented in the previous post using standalone mode websocket server on local dev environment.

1. create heroku

Sign up for heroku and install heroku tool. Follow the heroku setup instruction in the rails tutorial.

Now got gentle-tor-1752 for this entry.

2. redis setup

Because redis is used for the backend of websocket, we have to enable redis in heroku.

Then, check the redis setting information from heroku web UI (app -> redistogo). You will find info on redis db, such as below.

Next, apply these info on the redis setting.

If you don’t like write password into code, maybe you can use environment variables.

3. change websocket url

Change the websocket url specified in the code. Again, there could be better way to do this, but I put it into code simply.

4. small changes for heroku

put sqlite dev only and add pg and rails_12fator for prod env.

commit Gemfile.lock

set root path

5. deploy and check

entry.title: Initial Steps to Use Websocket-rails
entry.content:
This is the very initial steps to use websocket-rails. Basically, I follow the lessons from here. I also add some suplemental steps for smooth start and hopefully next steps to utilize this library.

1. prepare

2. modify controller

add controller inheriting WebsocketRails::BaseController. here, define websocket method “message_receive” which accepts message from client event and broadcast it to client connected with “websocket_chat”

3. routes

modify config/events.rb and map event “websocket_chat” to websocket method “message_receive”

4. generate chat app

5. define view

“trigger” sends data to event. “bind” subscribes message of the event specified on the first arg.

6. disable rack

if you use Websocket-Rails in development mode, you have to disable Rack::Lock.

7. boot local websocket

boot websocket server locally and set rails to allow local websocket. here, we use redis for its backend.

8. boot rails

9. access!

enjoy!

10. source

here is the repository of my sample.
